title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita

Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita plastic waste and GDP per capita for the year 2010, with GDP per capita expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale ranging from $1,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures plastic waste in kilograms per person per day from 0 kg to 0.6 kg. Data points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania, and circle sizes correspond to population. Countries with lower GDP per capita, such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Togo, and Tanzania, cluster in the bottom-left corner with waste generation rates generally below 0.1 kg. As GDP increases, the distribution of waste generation widens significantly. In the middle-income range between $5,000 and $20,000, countries like China, Indonesia, and Brazil show waste rates roughly between 0.05 kg and 0.15 kg, while nations like Sri Lanka and the Maldives reach higher values near 0.3 kg to 0.35 kg. The highest waste generation rates appear among specific island nations and wealthy economies; Antigua and Barbuda, Kuwait, and Guyana occupy the top of the chart with values exceeding 0.5 kg. Conversely, some high-GDP countries like Sweden, Romania, and Brunei maintain lower waste rates near or below 0.1 kg. Large population centers like India and China are represented by larger circles situated in the lower-to-middle sections of the waste scale.
